coredata.dc:description: As a synthetic estrogen, diethylstilbestrol (DES) residues in water pose a threat to human health. In this work, a novel electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) aptamer sensor based on NCDs@Ag3PO4 as a resonance energy transfer donor and Cu2+ doped Eu MOF (Cu: Eu MOF) as an efficient resonance energy transfer acceptor for the detection of diethylstilbestrol (DES) was proposed for the first time. The aptamer sensor uses Ag3PO4 nanoparticles loaded with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) as the ECL emitter, which improves the electron transfer efficiency of the sensor and promotes the generation of SO4 ·- radicals, thereby improving the luminescence intensity and stability of the ECL sensor. The energy acceptor Cu: Eu MOF binds to the apt on NCDs@Ag3PO4 by complementary base pairing through complementary DNA (cDNA). When DES is present in the sample, DES competes with the energy acceptor. The stronger interaction between DES and apt leads to the shedding of Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA from the electrode and the recovery of ECL signal. Based on this, under optimal conditions, the linear range of the sensor for detecting DES is 1.0 × 10−13–1.0 × 10−6 M, and the detection limit is 7.4 × 10−14 M (S/N = 3). The developed aptasensor showed excellent recognition ability for residual DES in actual water samples. The sensor has superior measurement ability and potential application value in the field of environmental water quality monitoring.

(A) CV curves and (B) EIS of different modified electrodes: (a) bare GCE; (b) Ag3PO4/GCE; (c) NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE; (d) apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE; (e) BSA/apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE; (f) Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA/BSA/apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE; (g) DES-Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA/BSA/apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE.

(D) Different modified electrodes: (a)GCE; (b) Ag3PO4/GCE; (c) NCDs@Ag3PO4/ GCE; (d) apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE; (e) BSA/apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE; (f)Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA/BSA/apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE; (g) DES-Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA/BSA/apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE ECL curve.

(A) CV responses of NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE in PBS (a) and Ag3PO4/GCE, NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE in PBS + 0.1 M K2S2O8. (B)ESR spectra of (a) NCDs@Ag3PO4 + DMPO + K2S₂O8, (b)Ag3PO4 + DMPO + K2S₂O8. (C) Possible ECL mechanism of NCDs@Ag3PO4 in PBS (pH = 8) containing 0.12 M K2S2O8. (D) ECL emission spectrum(D) of NCDs@Ag3PO4 and UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cu: Eu MOF.

The ECL intensity-time curves of different DES concentrations (DES concentration from a to j:1.0 × 10−13 M (a), 1.0 × 10−12 M (b), 1.0 × 10−11 M (c), 1.0 × 10−10 M (d), 1.0 × 10 −9 M (e), 1.0 × 10−8 M (f), 1.0 × 10−7 M (g), 1.0 × 10−6 M (h)). (B) The corresponding linear equation of the constructed ECL aptamer sensor for DES detection under a wide concentration range of 1 × 10−6 M ∼ 1 × 10−13 M. (C) Repeatability of 10 consecutive cycles scanned by the NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE (a), Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA/apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE (b). (D) Selectivity and specificity of the fabricated ECL aptasensor for DES detection (1 × 10−8) over other interferences (1 × 10−6).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a synthetic estrogen, diethylstilbestrol (DES) residues in water pose a threat to human health. In this work, a novel electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) aptamer sensor based on NCDs@Ag3PO4 as a resonance energy transfer donor and Cu2+ doped Eu MOF (Cu: Eu MOF) as an efficient resonance energy transfer acceptor for the detection of diethylstilbestrol (DES) was proposed for the first time. The aptamer sensor uses Ag3PO4 nanoparticles loaded with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) as the ECL emitter, which improves the electron transfer efficiency of the sensor and promotes the generation of SO4 ·- radicals, thereby improving the luminescence intensity and stability of the ECL sensor. The energy acceptor Cu: Eu MOF binds to the apt on NCDs@Ag3PO4 by complementary base pairing through complementary DNA (cDNA). When DES is present in the sample, DES competes with the energy acceptor. The stronger interaction between DES and apt leads to the shedding of Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA from the electrode and the recovery of ECL signal. Based on this, under optimal conditions, the linear range of the sensor for detecting DES is 1.0 × 10−13–1.0 × 10−6 M, and the detection limit is 7.4 × 10−14 M (S/N = 3). The developed aptasensor showed excellent recognition ability for residual DES in actual water samples. The sensor has superior measurement ability and potential application value in the field of environmental water quality monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental endocrine disruptors (EEDs) are a new class of persistent organic pollutants that interfere with the endocrine system [1]. Diethylstilbestrol (DES) is a teratogenic and carcinogenic EED, which is widely used as a growth stimulant and a drug for the treatment of pregnancy complications in animal husbandry [2]. However, diethylstilbestrol residues are easy to enter the water and soil environment, which not only poses a threat to the reproductive health of aquatic ecosystems and wildlife but also may affect higher organisms through the food chain, resulting in extensive ecological imbalance and loss of biodiversity [3]. Therefore, it is of great significance to control the environmental emission of diethylstilbestrol and rapidly detect the residual DES in water. The traditional methods for the detection of DES include enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [4], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [6], electrochemical (EC) and chemiluminescence (CL) [7] analysis. However, these techniques have complex pretreatment, cumbersome operation, and expensive equipment. Therefore, it is very important to establish a method for rapid and sensitive detection of DES.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a specific luminescence reaction that occurs on the electrode surface and is triggered by electrochemistry. It involves electrochemical and chemiluminescence processes. It has the characteristics of high sensitivity, low background, easy control, and short detection time. It has the advantages of both electrochemical and chemiluminescence methods [8]. Traditional ECL materials, such as luminol [9] and various semiconductor nanomaterials (CdTe [10] and CdSe [11]), have become an effective analytical method in electrochemical aptasensors due to their low price and good stability. However, the inherent toxicity of organic compounds and heavy metal elements limits their application. Therefore, it is necessary to find electroluminescent materials with good biocompatibility and environmental friendliness. Silver orthophosphate (Ag3PO4) is a semiconductor with low toxicity and good biocompatibility. It has good ECL performance in the presence of K2S2O8 [12]. In addition, as a zero-dimensional carbon-based luminescent material, carbon quantum dots (CDs) are widely used in the construction of ECL sensors [8] due to their non-toxicity, good biocompatibility, high quantum yield, and adjustable fluorescence. However, the ECL emission of pure carbon quantum dots is weak and the excitation potential is high, which limits its application in sensors. Studies have shown that by doping heteroatoms in CDs, the surface traps of quantum dots can be increased, and the electron transfer between the co-reactant and the quantum dots can be promoted, thereby significantly enhancing its ECL emission [13]. For example, Han et al. [14] constructed an electrochemiluminescence sensor using nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) as luminophores to detect neuron-specific enolase (NSE). Compared with CDs, NCDs with nitrogen introduced have higher ECL emissions and improved the sensitivity of the sensor. The detection limit is 6.30 fg/mL. Therefore, using the synergistic effect of Ag3PO4 and NCDs, the combination of NCDs and Ag3PO4 can exhibit a stronger ECL signal than the monomer.

Recently, ECL sensors based on resonance energy transfer (RET) strategy have attracted more and more attention. Due to its significant advantages, including a convenient analysis process, high sensitivity, no additional light source, and low background noise, ECL-RET has been widely studied for the analysis of water pollutants. In order to modulate the ECL emission of NCDs@Ag3PO4 at an appropriate level to indicate the weak concentration change of the analyte, so as to achieve higher sensitivity, it is necessary to find a suitable quenching probe. Metal-organic framework (MOF) is a porous crystalline material formed by coordinating organic ligands with metals [15]. MOFs have a wide range of structural diversity due to the extensive combination of metals (or metal clusters) and different types of organic ligands. In recent years, metal-doped metal-organic frameworks (MDMOF) have attracted more and more attention. Due to the competition of various metal cations for ligands, metal doping has changed the coordination type of organic ligands and central metal ions. Doping can improve its performance including increasing active sites, increasing specific surface area, optimizing electrochemical and optical properties, and providing more possibilities for MOF development [16]. As a member of the MOF family, lanthanide metal-organic framework materials (Ln-MOFs) are widely known for their excellent luminescence properties [17]. Their main luminescence properties are derived from f-f transitions, but these transitions are usually located in the visible or near-infrared region rather than the ultraviolet region. Therefore, the ultraviolet absorption of the lanthanide elements themselves is relatively weak. However, studies have shown that the band gap of MOF can be effectively reduced by using electron-rich Cu2+, Co2+, or Ni2+ as metal nodes to absorb visible light [18]. Therefore, in this paper, Cu2+ is introduced into Eu MOF for the first time, and its regulation of the long wave range of absorption spectrum is successfully realized.

In this paper, an electrochemiluminescence sensor based on resonance energy transfer (RET) mechanism was successfully prepared by using Ag3PO4 nanoparticles loaded with NCDs as energy donors (NCDs@Ag3PO4) and Cu2+ doped Eu MOF as energy acceptors (Cu: Eu MOF). The aptamer (apt) of DES is firmly adsorbed on the luminophore material NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE through π-π conjugation [19], which can specifically recognize DES in the sample. Through the ‘on-off-on’ strategy, the aptamer sensor has been successfully applied to the specific detection of DES in real water samples, and has excellent detection ability and wide application prospect in the field of life health and water environment monitoring.

NaH2PO4·12H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O, potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), silver nitrate (AgNO3), anhydrous ethanol, potassium chloride, citric acid C6H8O7, urea, isophthalic acid (IPA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.; europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O) was purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium citrate, lincomycin, chloramphenicol, tetracycline, enrofloxacin, norfloxacin were purchased from Aladdin; potassium ferricyanide was purchased from Saen Chemical Technology Co., Ltd.; chloramphenicol was purchased from Shanghai Bioengineering Co., Ltd. All reagents were analytically pure and not further purified when used. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm, 25 °C) was used in all experiments.

Diethylstilbestrol aptamer was purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. The DNA sequence was: 5′-(NH2 C6)-GGC GAT GGG GTA GGG GGT GTG GAG GGG CCG GAC GGA GGG G-3′) and cDNA (5′-(NH2 C6)-CCC-CTC-CGT-CCG-GCC-CCT-CCA-3).

The morphology of the material was characterized by a JEOL scanning electron microscope (JSM-6360LA, Japan). The UV–visible absorption spectrum of the material was tested by UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and the Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) of the material was tested by NICOLET IN10 (Thermo Fisher, USA). The materials were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using Max2000 (Japan) and AXIS-Ultra DLD (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. The ECL test was performed using the LK5100Plus multifunctional ECL system (Tianjin Lanlike Chemical Electronics Co., Ltd., China). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were performed using the CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd., China).

NCDs were prepared by slightly changing the hydrothermal method reported in the literature [20]. Firstly, 3.000 g citric acid and 1.000 g urea were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water and stirred at room temperature for 30 min to form a uniform solution. Then, the solution was transferred to the reactor, reacted at 180 °C for 5 h, cooled to 25 °C, centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was dialyzed. Finally, the NCDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying the obtained solution.

NCDs (0.0010, 0.0020, 0.0030, 0.0040, 0.0040, 0.0050, 0.0060 g) and PVP (0.5000 g) were dispersed in 50 mL ultrapure water, and then 0.2000 g AgNO3 and 1.0650 g Na2HPO4 solution were added under stirring. The mixture solution was stirred in the dark for 4 h and centrifuged at 5000 rpm. The obtained products were washed three times with ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. NCDs@Ag3PO4 with different amounts of NCDs were obtained (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Cu: Eu MOF and u: Eu MOF-cDNA
ce:section.ce:para: Eu (NO3)3·6H2O (0.0290 g) and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (0.0870 g) were dissolved in 24 mL water and added to 30 mL N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), then mixed with 12 mL IPA (0.0797 g) in DMF for 20 min. The mixture was transferred to a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene stainless steel autoclave. After reacting at 160 °C for 6 h, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to separate the precipitate, and washed three times with ethanol. The product was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain Cu: Eu MOF. Cu MOF and Eu MOF were prepared without adding Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, respectively.

100 μL of cDNA Tris-H3PO4 solution was added to 1 mL of Cu: Eu MOF (1 mg/mL) aqueous solution. After incubation at 37 °C for 12 h, the solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to remove excess cDNA, and the resulting precipitate was re-dispersed in 1 mL of aqueous solution. The solution was sonicated for 15 min, then shaken at 25 °C for 2 h, and finally stored at 4 °C for later use.

The preparation process of the sensor is shown in Scheme 1 . The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with 0.3 μm and 0.05 μm Al2O3 powder in turn, ultrasonically cleaned with dilute nitric acid, anhydrous ethanol and deionized water for 3 min, respectively, and then dried naturally. 5 μL 1 mg/mL NCDs@Ag3PO4 was dropped on the surface of the clean GCE and dried under an infrared lamp to obtain NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE. Then 5 μL 3 μM apt was added to the surface of the modified electrode and incubated at 37 °C for 6 h to obtain apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE. Then, 5 μL 3 μM Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA was dropped onto the electrode surface and fully combined with apt at 37 °C for 30 min. Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA/apt-NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE was obtained and an electrochemiluminescence aptasensor was constructed.

The prepared sensor was subjected to an ECL test using a three-electrode system. Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA/BSA/apt-NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE was used as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode, and platinum wire was used as the counter electrode. The three electrodes were placed in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 8.0) containing 0.12 M K2S2O8, the scanning voltage range was 0.0 v ∼ −1.7 V, the scanning speed was 100 mV/s, and the photomultiplier tube high voltage was set to 800 V.

When detecting the DES of the sample, the prepared Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA/BSA/apt-NCDs@Ag3PO4 modified GCE was enriched in the solution for 30 min, and then the ECL intensity was recorded by an electrochemiluminescence instrument. The change value is marked as ΔIECL (ΔIECL = I-I0), where I0 is the ECL intensity of Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA/BSA/apt-NCDs@Ag3PO4 modified GCE without DES in the sample, and I is the ECL intensity in the presence of different concentrations of DES.

The morphology of Ag3PO4 and NCDs@Ag3PO4 was studied by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). It can be seen from Fig. 1A that the synthesized Ag3PO4 has an irregular spherical morphology with an average diameter of about 200 nm. The NCDs@Ag3PO4 obtained by simple stirring of NCDs and Ag3PO4 are shown in Fig. 1B. The NCDs are well distributed on the surface of Ag3PO4 (as shown in the red circle section of the picture). In addition, the mapping images of C, O, N, P, and Ag elements can be clearly observed on the mapping images of NCDs@Ag3PO4 (Fig. 1(D–H)). SEM and EDS-mapping analysis proved that NCDs @ Ag3PO4 composites were successfully prepared.

The XRD characterization results of NCDs and NCDs@Ag3PO4 are shown in Fig. 2A. According to the XRD spectra of NCDs, a broad absorption peak around 24° can be observed, which is due to the presence of disordered carbon atoms [21]. The XRD pattern of NCDs@Ag3PO4 composites is consistent with that of Ag3PO4. However, because XRD cannot distinguish a small amount of fine and highly dispersed NCDs, no diffraction peak of NCDs is found in the spectrum of NCDs@Ag3PO4. Fig. 2B shows the FT-IR spectra of the prepared materials. Among them, the peak of NCDs (Fig. 2B(a)) at 1588–1704 cm−1 belongs to CO stretching vibration; the peak at 1554 cm−1 is attributed to the NH bending vibration and CN stretching vibration of -CONH-, and the peak at 1206–1452 cm−1 is attributed to the CN stretching vibration, NH bending vibration and CO stretching vibration, indicating the presence of carbonyl and amino groups [22,23], it shows the successful preparation of NCDs. Ag3PO4 (Fig. 2B(c)) exhibits a characteristic peak at 908 cm−1, corresponding to the PO stretching vibration of phosphate (PO4 3−) [24]. In the FT-IR spectrum of NCDs@Ag3PO4 (Fig. 2B(b)), the peak at 1548 cm−1 is attributed to NH bond and CN bond, the peak at 1052 cm−1 is attributed to CO bond, and the peak at 928 cm−1 is attributed to PO bond, indicating that NCDs@Ag3PO4 has both the absorption peak of NCDs and the characteristic absorption peak of Ag3PO4, it is preliminarily proved that Ag3PO4 nanoparticles are successfully loaded with NCDs.

In the Raman spectrum of NCDs (Fig. 2C), the carbon D band and D′ band are observed at 1309 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1, which are caused by the defect degree of sp3 carbon atom. The carbon G band is observed at 1560 cm−1, and the G band is attributed to sp2 carbon atom [25]；In the Raman spectra of Ag3PO4, the lattice vibration of the AgO bond was observed at 242 cm−1, and the characteristic peaks of Ag3PO4 were observed at 551 cm−1 and 914 cm−1, which were attributed to the asymmetric bending vibration and symmetric stretching vibration of the phosphorus‑oxygen bond (PO) within the PO4 3− group, respectively [26]. In the Raman spectrum of NCDs@Ag3PO4, the corresponding characteristic peaks were also observed, which proved that NCDs were successfully loaded on Ag3PO4 nanoparticles. XPS was used to analyze the chemical properties and composition of the surface in NCDs@Ag3PO4, and the results were shown in Fig. 1D. The peaks at 284.28 eV and 399.18 eV are attributed to C 1s and N 1s (Fig. S1A ∼ B) [27], respectively, which proves NCDs were successfully loaded on Ag3PO4 nanoparticles. In addition, the peaks at 530.78 eV and 132.38 eV are attributed to O 1 s and P 2p, respectively (Fig. S1C ∼ D), and Ag 3d peaks are observed at 374.48 eV and 366.28 eV (Fig. S1E) [28]. The above morphology, composition, crystal structure, and spectral analysis proved the successful preparation of NCDs@Ag3PO4.

Firstly, the micro-morphology of Cu MOF and Eu MOF was characterized. The results are shown in Fig. S2. It can be seen from the observation that Cu MOF exhibits nanowire aggregates with a diameter of ≈ 20 nm (Fig. S2(A)), and Eu MOF exhibits a hollow spherical structure with a diameter of ≈ 700 nm and a loose surface (Fig. S2(B)). The morphology of Cu: Eu MOF is shown in Fig. 3A. The morphology of Cu: Eu MOF is hollow spherical particles with a diameter of about 500 nm. Due to the small doping amount of Cu2+, the morphology is closer to Eu MOF. Compared with Eu MOF, Cu: Eu MOF becomes smaller and the reduction of surface defects becomes denser. In addition, through EDS analysis, it can be seen that Cu: Eu MOF contains Cu element and Eu element, among which Cu element accounts for 20.4 At.%, which preliminarily proves the successful doping of Cu2+ (Fig. 3B).

It can be seen from the XRD analysis of Fig. 3C that for Cu MOF (curve a), the diffraction peaks at 2θ angles of 29.5°, 36.4°, 42.3°, 61.3°, 73.5° and 77.3° correspond to the (110), (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) planes of Cu MOF, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 C curve b, the ‘steamed bread'-like protrusions of Eu MOF near 2θ angles of 10° and 20° indicate that it has an amorphous structure, which is consistent with the results of Wang et al. [29]. Compared with Eu MOF and Cu MOF (PDF # 05–0667), the XRD results (curve c) of Cu: Eu MOF are similar to those of Eu MOF, and there are slightly shifted diffraction peaks of Cu MOF near 43° and 74°. This may be due to the fact that some Cu atoms replace Eu atoms, and the radius of Cu2+ is less than the radius of Eu3+, resulting in lattice shrinkage and the distance between planes decreases, which eventually leads to the peak shift of Cu: Eu MOF [30]. This phenomenon indicates that Cu in-situ replaces Eu in the Eu MOF lattice, which proves that the expected Cu: Eu MOF was successfully synthesized in this experiment. The above XRD results further illustrate the successful doping of Cu2+ in Eu-MOF.

Electrochemical cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of different modified electrodes were tested in a three-electrode system to analyze the electrochemical behavior of each electrode. The CV test was carried out with 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4-as a probe at −0.2–0.6 V, the scanning speed was 100 mV/s, the results are shown in Fig. 4A, the bare GCE (curve a) has a pair of good redox peaks; compared with bare GCE, the reduction peak potential of Ag3PO4/GCE (curve b) shifted positively from 0 v to about 0.12 V, and the reduction peak current decreased from −95 μA to −57 μA. This is because Ag3PO4, as an inorganic semiconductor, has the characteristic of hindering electron transfer, which reduces the electron transfer rate. After compounding Ag3PO4 with NCDs, the peak current of NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE (curve c) increased to −73 μA, and the reduction peak potential shifted positively to 0.09 v. This is because the introduction of NCDs shortens the electron transport path, thereby enhancing the overall electron transport efficiency [31]; The reduction peak current of apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE (curve d) is lower than that of NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE, because the aptamer is a non-conductive biological macromolecule that hinders electron transfer. After further modification of BSA, the reduction peak current was further reduced due to the fact that biomolecules hindered electron transfer on the electrode surface (curve e). When Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA was further modified on BSA/apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE (curve f), the reduction peak current of Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA/BSA/apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE was further reduced to −55 μA. Finally, when the aptamer sensor was incubated in 10−8 M DES solution for 30 min, the Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA fell off from the aptamer sensor due to the specific recognition of the aptamer to DES, and the peak current increased (curve g).

In addition, the EIS results are shown in Fig. 4B. The bare GCE has the smallest resistance (curve a, 108 Ω). When Ag3PO4 is modified on the surface of the electrode, the impedance of the modified electrode increases to 3174.2 Ω (curve b), which is due to the fact that Ag3PO4 as an inorganic semiconductor has the characteristics of hindering electron transfer, resulting in an increase in the impedance of the modified electrode. When NCDs@Ag3PO4 were modified on the surface of the electrode, the impedance of the modified electrode was 373.2 Ω (curve c), which further indicated that NCDs@Ag3PO4 had good electron transfer ability. After the aptamer and BSA were modified on the electrode surface, the resistance increased to 2395 Ω (curve d) and 3319 Ω (curve e), respectively, because the biomolecules hindered the electron transfer on the electrode surface. Further addition of Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA to the electrode significantly increased the resistance to 4378 Ω (curve f). Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA/BSA/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE was incubated in PBS solution containing 1 × 10−8 M DES for 30 min. Due to the stronger binding force between DES and apt, Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA fell off from the electrode surface, resulting in a decrease in resistance to 3503 Ω (curve g). The EIS results were consistent with the trend of the above CV results, which proved the preparation of ECL sensor based on resonance energy transfer strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL performance of the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 (A–C) is the ECL curves of different modified electrodes (Ag3PO4/GCE, NCDs/GCE, NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE) scanned for 10 cycles in the potential range of-1.7 V ∼ 0 V. Fig. 5A is the ECL curve of Ag3PO4/GCE. The ECL intensity of Ag3PO4/GCE is about 22,000 a.u., and the ECL intensity changes little after 10 cycles of scanning, and the RSD is 2.46 %. This is because the semiconductor properties of Ag3PO4 can effectively generate and separate electron-hole pairs, thereby promoting efficient ECL and having excellent stability. [32]. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 5B that the ECL intensity of NCDs/GCE decreased significantly with the extension of time, from around 35,000 a.u. to around 4000 a.u. This is due to the better hydrophilicity of NCDs, which is easy to fall off from the electrode surface and have poor stability when tested in an aqueous solution [33]. After loading NCDs on Ag3PO4 nanoparticles, the ECL stability of NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE was improved and the intensity was significantly increased, which was about 50,000 a.u., and the RSD of the ECL response was 3.26 % (Fig. 5C) after 10 cycles of scanning. This is because the amino (−NH2) and carboxyl (-COOH) on the surface of NCDs can interact with the oxygen atoms on the surface of Ag3PO4 by hydrogen bonding, which significantly enhances the binding force between the two [34]. Secondly, due to the existence of a variety of active sites on the surface of Ag3PO4, these sites can further promote the stable adhesion of NCDs on its surface through physical adsorption and electrostatic interaction, which solves the problem that it is easy to fall off during the detection of aqueous solution and improves the ECL stability of the material. In addition, the synergistic effect of the two effectively promotes electron transfer and improves the ECL intensity of the material.

In order to explore the feasibility of the prepared sensor for the detection of DES, the modified electrode was subjected to an ECL test, and the obtained ECL signal was recorded and displayed in Fig. 5D. As can be seen from the diagram, the ECL intensity of bare GCE is ≈ 1500 a.u. (curve a). The ECL intensity of Ag3PO4/GCE was ≈ 22,000 a.u. (curve b). The ECL intensity of NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE is ≈ 50,000 a.u. (curve c). This indicates that NCDs@Ag3PO4 are modified on the electrode and have a strong ECL signal. After further modification of apt and BSA, the ECL intensity decreased to ≈ 40,000 a.u. (curve d) and 37,000 a.u.(curve e). Subsequently, the acceptor Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA was dropped on the electrode surface, and the ECL intensity decreased significantly to 15,000 a.u. (curve f). This is because the donor NCDs@Ag3PO4 and the acceptor Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA are combined by base complementary pairing, which shortens the distance between the two and triggers resonance energy transfer, which weakens the ECL intensity of NCDs@Ag3PO4. Finally, the prepared sensor was enriched in a DES solution with a concentration of 1 × 10−8 M for 30 min, and the electrode was taken out and the excess DES was washed away with PBS, and the ECL value was tested to be ≈ 30,000 a.u. (curve g). This is because apt specifically binds to the analyte DES and replaces the weaker force between apt and cDNA. The Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA falls off from the electrode, the resonance energy transfer effect disappears, and the ECL intensity rebounds. The above results indicate that the electrochemiluminescence sensor is successfully prepared and can be used for the detection of DES.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the mechanism of luminescence and quenching
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prove the mechanism of NCDs@Ag3PO4 and K2S2O8, NCDs@Ag3PO4 was subjected to cyclic voltammetry in pure PBS and PBS containing 0.12 M K2S2O8, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6A, the reduction peak of NCDs@Ag3PO4 did not appear in PBS without K2S2O8 (curve a). In PBS containing K2S2O8, the reduction peak of NCDs@Ag3PO4 appeared at-0.6 V (curve c), indicating that K2S2O8 was a co-reactant of NCDs@Ag3PO4.

In order to explore the effect of Ag3PO4 nanoparticles loaded with NCDs on the ECL luminescence performance, the CV curves of Ag3PO4 and NCDs@Ag3PO4 in PBS solution containing 0.12 M K2S2O8 were tested respectively. As shown in Fig. 6A, the peak reduction potential of K2S2O8 is −0.7 V (curve b). After the introduction of NCDs, the peak reduction potential of K2S2O8 is −0.6 V (curve c), showing a significant positive shift, indicating that NCDs can effectively promote the electrochemical reaction of K2S2O8 on the electrode surface.

Finally, the ESR spectrum of NCDs@Ag3PO4/K2S2O8 system was tested using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as a free radical scavenger (Fig. 6B). Ag3PO4 was tested in the mixture of DMPO and S2O8 2−. The 1:2:2:1 quartet mode characteristic peak of the DMPO-OH• adduct and the weak characteristic peak of the DMPO-SO4 •- adduct appeared, which means the production of SO4 •- (free radical ions) and OH• (hydroxyl radical) in the solution at this time (curve a) [35,36]. The NCDs@Ag3PO4 was tested in the mixture of DMPO and S2O8 2− to obtain the characteristic peaks of DMPO-OH• adducts and DMPO-SO4 •- adducts stronger than Ag3PO4 (curve b). It is indicated that the loading of NCDs on the surface of Ag3PO4 nanoparticles can effectively promote the production of more OH• and SO4 •- (Eq. (2)), thereby further improving the luminescence performance of the ECL sensor. The above results are consistent with the results obtained in the CV test. Finally, the possible reaction mechanism in the NCDs@Ag3PO4/K2S2O8 system is shown in Fig. 6C, and the specific reaction equations can be expressed as follows (Eqs. (1)–(4)):

In order to further prove the efficient quenching effect of the immunosensor caused by Cu2+ doping, as shown in Fig. S4, the apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE electrode was used as the initial ECL probe (curve a). Then, Cu MOF-cDNA, Eu MOF-cDNA, and Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA were further modified on the prepared electrode, respectively. The ECL intensity of Cu MOF-cDNA/apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE (curve b) was about 22,000 a.u.; the ECL intensity of Eu MOF-cDNA/apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE (curve c) was about 30,000 a.u.; the ECL intensity of Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA/apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE (curve d) was about 15,000 a.u., Compared with the ECL signal of NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE, the quenching efficiency reached 70 %, which proved the good quenching effect of Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA. Based on this, in order to verify the quenching effect of the acceptor Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA on the donor NCDs@Ag3PO4, the ECL spectrum of NCDs@Ag3PO4 and the UV absorption spectrum of Cu: Eu MOF were tested respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 6D that there is a good overlap between the two in the range of 400–600 nm. Secondly, the receptor Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA and apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/ GCE bind stably through the base complementary pairing between cDNA and apt, which shortens the distance between the donor and the receptor and ensures the effectiveness of RET. Compared with the ECL signal of NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE (50,000 a.u.), the ECL intensity of Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA/apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE (Fig. S4(curve d)) was about 15,000 a.u. The above results confirm the occurrence of resonance energy transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions and analytical performance of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain better detection results, the experimental results are optimized. The detailed optimization content is shown in Supplementary Material, and the results are shown in Fig. S5. Since the constructed ECL sensor uses NCDs@Ag3PO4 as the luminophore, the additional amount of NCDs will directly affect the ECL performance of the sensor. Therefore, the effects of different addition amounts of NCDs on the ECL performance of NCDs@Ag3PO4 composites were investigated. As shown in Fig. S5A, as the amount of NCDs added increases from 1 mg to 4 mg, the ECL gradually increases. This may be due to the increase in the amount of NCDs added, more NCDs are fixed on the surface of Ag3PO4. The addition of NCDs improves the conductivity of NCDs@Ag3PO4 and promotes more SO4 •- production, resulting in an increased ECL response [37]. When the amount of modification was further increased from 4 mg to 6 mg, ECL decreased. This may be due to the excessive amount of NCDs on Ag3PO4, which hindered the electron transfer rate of Ag3PO4 and affected the synergistic effect of the two, resulting in a decrease in ECL. When the addition amount is 4 mg, the ECL is the largest, about 50,000 a.u. Therefore, the optimal addition amount of NCDs in NCDs@Ag3PO4 is 4 mg.

Since the co-reactant K2S2O8 will directly affect the ECL intensity of the material, the effects of different concentrations of K2S2O8 on the sensor were investigated. The results are shown in Fig. S5B. When the concentration of K2S2O8 increases from 0.04 M to 0.12 M, the ECL shows an increasing trend. This is due to the fact that more co-reactants will produce more SO4 •-, which will react with NCDs@Ag3PO4 •- to produce more NCDs@Ag3PO4 ⁎; when the concentration of K2S2O8 continues to increase from 0.12 M to 0.14 M, ECL decreases. This is because when the concentration of K2S2O8 is too high, there will be excessive S2O8 2−. S2O8 2− will react with NCDs@Ag3PO4 •-, consuming part of NCDs@Ag3PO4 •-. The decrease of NCDs@Ag3PO4 •- will affect the formation of excited state NCDs@Ag3PO4 ⁎, and ECL decreases [37]. When the concentration of K2S2O8 is 0.12 M, the maximum ECL is about 50,000 a.u. Therefore, the optimum concentration of K2S2O8 is 0.12 M.

The pH value of the buffer solution plays an important role in the construction of the aptamer sensor. The effect of the pH value of the buffer solution on ECL is shown in Fig. S5C. It can be seen from the figure that the ECL value gradually increased in the range of pH = 5.0–8.0, reached a maximum of about 50,000 a.u. At pH = 8.0, and then decreased with the increase of pH. This is mainly because when the pH is less than 8.0, the protons around the electrode are easily reduced to H2, which inhibits the reduction of S2O8 2−, resulting in a decrease in the production of SO4 •-, and ultimately reduces the ECL intensity [38]. Under alkaline conditions, excessive anions are dispersed around the electrode, thereby preventing SO4 •- from approaching the electrode surface, resulting in a decrease in ECL signal [39]. Therefore, the pH value of the whole experimental process was selected to be 8.

Since the scanning rate will affect the reaction between the luminophore and the co-reactant, the effect of different scanning rates on the sensor was investigated. As shown in Fig. S5D, when the scanning rate increases from 50 mV·s−1 to 100 mV·s−1, the ECL increases. This is because the too-low scanning rate makes the intermediate NCDs@Ag3PO4 ⁎ less, so increasing the scanning rate will promote the formation of NCDs@Ag3PO4 ⁎, resulting in an increase in ECL; when the scan rate further increases from 100 mV·s−1 to 175 mV·s−1, the ECL intensity decreases. This is because the consumption rate of K2S2O8 on the electrode surface is higher than the diffusion rate of the solution to the electrode surface, resulting in a lower instantaneous concentration of K2S2O8 on the electrode surface [37]. ECL reaches the maximum (≈ 30,000 a.u.) at a scan rate of 100 mV·s−1, so the scan rate selected in the experiment is 100 mV·s−1.

The concentration of aptamer also plays an important role in the whole detection process. As shown in Fig. S5E, the ECL value increased with the increase of aptamer concentration. When the aptamer concentration increased to 3 μM, the ECL change value tended to be stable, so the aptamer concentration was selected to be 3 μM.

The relationship between the binding time of DES aptamer and ECL was studied. The concentration of DES was 1 × 10−8 M and the concentration of aptamer was 3 μM. As shown in Fig. S5F, ΔECL increases first and then decreases slowly with the increase of binding time, and the ΔECL value is the largest when the binding time is 30 min. Therefore, 30 min was selected as the binding time of aptamer and DES.

Subsequently, under optimal conditions, the prepared sensor was used to determine the standard solution of different DES concentrations. The final results are shown in Fig. 7A and Fig. 7B. It can be seen from Fig. 7A that the ECL value of the sensor decreased with the decrease of the concentration of DES in the range of 1 × 10−13–1 × 10−6 M. This is because with the increase of DES concentration, DES gradually replaces the binding of Cu:Eu MOF-cDNA to apt. This specific binding leads to the shedding of Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA from the electrode surface. The resonance energy transfer effect gradually weakens until it disappears, and the ECL intensity rebounds. The ECL change value (ΔECL) of the sensor showed a good linear relationship with the logarithmic change value of DES concentration (Fig. 7B). The linear regression equation was ΔECL = 41,738.15 + 2966.34 × lgC, R2 = 0.9968, and the detection limit was 7.4 × 10−14 M (3σ/slope). Compared with other detection methods, the proposed ECL sensor has satisfactory application potential in the detection of DES in water (Table S1).

The stability of the prepared electrochemiluminescence sensor was studied by 10 consecutive scans, and the results were shown in Fig. 7C. From the graph, NCDs@Ag3PO4 modified on the bare glassy carbon electrode showed a stable and strong ECL performance (curve a), and the obtained ECL value was ≈ 50,000 a.u., RSD = 1.99 %. This lays a good foundation for the further preparation of aptamer sensors with stable ECL performance. In practical applications, the selectivity of the ECL sensor is also crucial. Lincomycin (Lin), chloramphenicol (CAP), enrofloxacin (ENR), tetracycline (TC), and norfloxacin (NOR), which are common pollutants in water environment, were selected as interfering substances. The results are shown in Fig. 7D. The concentration of the interference was 10−6 M, and the concentration of DES was 10−8 M. Under the condition that the concentration of the interference was 100 times the concentration of DES, the change of ΔECL was smaller than that without the interference, and the change value was about 500 a.u., indicating that these interferences had no significant effect on the detection of DES by the sensor, which proved that the prepared electrochemiluminescence aptamer sensor had very good selectivity.

Reproducibility is also very important for evaluating the performance of the sensor. Therefore, the ECL of six groups of NCDs@Ag3PO4 and the prepared aptamer sensors were tested under the same experimental conditions. The test results are shown in Fig. S6A. Curve a is the ECL intensity value of the six groups of NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE, the intensity is about 50,000 a.u., and the RSD is 2.19 %. The curve b is the ECL value of the six groups of aptamer sensors, the intensity is about 15,000 a.u., and the RSD is 1.12 %, indicating that both NCDs@Ag3PO4 and aptamer sensors have high reproducibility. In addition, the long-term stability of the sensor was tested (Fig. S6B). After 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 days of storage, the ECL value of NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE was 100 %, 99.90 %, 96.48 %, 96.14 % and 96.13 % of the initial intensity value, while the ECL value of the aptamer sensor Cu: Eu MOF-cDNA/apt/NCDs@Ag3PO4/GCE based on RET strategy was 100 %, 99.47 %, 99.45 %, 99.30 % and 98.33 % of the initial value. It shows that the prepared sensor has good long-term stability.

In order to test the practical application value of the sensor, the standard addition method was used to detect DES in tap water and pond water. No DES was found in the actual tap water and pond water, and then the recovery experiment of DES was carried out. The results are shown in Table 1 . The recovery rate was between 95.8 % and 100.5 %, and the RSD of the test results was less than 5 %. The above results showed that the constructed aptamer sensor had potential applicability to the analysis of DES content in actual samples.

In summary, an electrochemiluminescence aptamer sensor for sensitive detection of DES was successfully prepared by using the ‘ON-OFF-ON’ mechanism. NCDs@Ag3PO4 material was used as the donor of resonance energy transfer, and Ag3PO4 nanoparticles were used to load NCDs to improve the luminescence intensity and stability of the sensor. The synergistic effect of NCDs@Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4 nanoparticles showed excellent electrochemical luminescence performance. In addition, Cu2+ doped Eu MOF was prepared for the first time as a receptor material for resonance energy transfer. The doping of Cu2+ regulates the absorption spectrum of Cu: Eu MOF and ensures efficient resonance energy transfer. The aptasensor based on the resonance energy transfer mechanism was used for the detection of DES. There was a good linear relationship between the ECL change value (ΔECL) and the aptamer sensor in the range of 10−6–10−13 M, and the detection limit was 7.4 × 10−14 M (3σ/slope). The sensor has good reproducibility, stability, and long-term stability, and can be used for specific and sensitive detection of DES in actual samples. It has superior measurement capabilities and good application prospects in the field of environmental water quality monitoring.

Longmei Yao: Writing – original draft. Jiajia Zhi: Writing – original draft. Wenchang Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Qingyi Li: Writing – review & editing. Ding Jiang: Writing – review & editing. Xiaohui Chen: Writing – review & editing. Zhidong Chen: Supervision, Funding acquisition.
